sections.paragraph: an unused root; to flame, to shine, Gr. λάμπω. The original idea is in lapping, being lambent, lambendo, which the Phœnicio-Shemites and the Greeks express by the syllable lab, lap (לָאַב, לָהַב labium, لب Lippe, see page 434, B ), and apply to flame. The common root therefore of the Hebrew and the Greek verb is לף, to which a third radical ד is added, in the same manner as to עִם, עִמָּד, Gr. ὅμος, ὅμοδος; and a vestige of this is found in the Greek λαμπάζ, Gen. λαμπάδοζ. From the Phœnicio-Shemitic לַפֵּד, inflected in the Aramean manner לַמְפֵּד, springs the Gr. λάμπω; and from לַפִּיר, Syr. ܠܰܡܦܝܺܕܐܳ, Ch. לַמְפַּד, Gr. λαμπάζ, λαμπάδοζ, and not contrariwise.-I formerly compared this with لد to shine, on the authority of Castell; but this verb is spurious in this sense, and must be regarded as a mistake of that lexicographer. According to the Kamûs, page 417, it denotes nothing but depression, lowness. Hence-
